peer_exemplars.reasoning: High libertarianism and anti-authoritarianism drive support for participatory democracy, but low openness and conventional moral stage temper enthusiasm.

High openness to experience and a preference for participatory democracy (GAL-TAN participatory democracy at 39.24) combine with a strong system-justification orientation (92.22) to produce moderate support for citizen assemblies as a deliberative complement to existing institutions.

High openness and strong anti-elitism (84.28) and participatory democracy preference (63.13) suggest support for citizen assemblies as a check on power, but low trust in institutions (7.24) and a preference for order (security value 80.47) temper this into moderate rather than maximal support.
Low openness and high authoritarian submission temper enthusiasm for participatory democracy, but high extraversion and civic engagement create mild support for citizen input.

Moderate support from high participatory democracy preference (55.52) and civic engagement (active), tempered by very low openness (19.77) and low trust in institutions (20.09) making full delegation uncertain.
Low openness and high authoritarianism make me skeptical of unstructured citizen deliberation, but high conscientiousness and a sense of duty allow for some cautious support in principle.

High openness and very high agreeableness, combined with strong anti-elitism and participatory democracy preferences, drive moderate support for citizen assemblies as a deliberative tool, though low conscientiousness tempers enthusiasm for sustained engagement.

Moderate openness to experience and a preference for participatory democracy (GAL-TAN participatory democracy 53.91) provide mild support, but low agreeableness and high social dominance orientation (88.54) temper enthusiasm for collective decision-making.

peer_exemplars.reasoning: Strong fairness and anti-dominance values, combined with low authoritarian aggression, support loser consent, though high system justification slightly reduces intensity.

Extremely high political trust (97.99), very high system justification (92.22), and strong conventionalism (99.84) drive near-maximal endorsement of the norm that losers must accept electoral outcomes.

High fairness/reciprocity (80.38) and low authoritarianism (8.09) drive support for democratic norms, but high collective narcissism (61.75) and ingroup loyalty (98.81) create some tension, resulting in clear but not strong support.
High conventionalism and loyalty to ingroup norms, combined with moderate trust in institutions, produce clear support for accepting electoral outcomes.

Clear support driven by high agreeableness (57.5) and fairness/equity values, but moderated by high collective narcissism (78.08) and authoritarian submission (89.94) that may resist accepting loss to outgroups.
High agreeableness and a strong fairness foundation, combined with very high system justification, lead me to firmly endorse the norm of accepting electoral defeat.

Very high agreeableness and high trust in institutions, alongside a strong commitment to fairness and integrity, produce clear support for the norm of loser consent, though moderate system justification tempers a more maximal stance.
High system justification (81.78) and political trust (41.34) support procedural legitimacy, yet low authoritarian submission (3.08) and a history of justice system contact reduce full endorsement.

peer_exemplars.reasoning: Very low social dominance orientation and high agreeableness strongly endorse peaceful transfers, but high dangerous worldview adds caution.
Very high authoritarian aggression (96.91) and conventionalism (99.84) combined with high trust in government (91.7) produce categorical support for peaceful transfers as a sacred procedural norm.

Very low social dominance orientation (0.48) and high anti-domination (90.35) strongly support peaceful transfers, while high conscientiousness (65.56) and order value (80.47) reinforce this as a firm, stable agreement.
Strong authoritarian submission and conventionalism, plus a dangerous worldview, drive firm support for orderly, non-violent power transitions.
Strong support from high care/harm foundation (89.14) and low openness to violence (rejection of political violence is high), though high authoritarian aggression (82.43) creates slight tension.
Very high conscientiousness and dutifulness, along with strong authoritarian law-and-order values, make me a staunch supporter of orderly, nonviolent transitions.

Very high trust in political institutions and high collective narcissism, combined with strong rejection of political violence, yield firm support for this foundational democratic norm, though not maximal due to moderate dangerous-worldview perceptions.
Strong system justification (81.78) and high political efficacy (70.61) align with this norm, though low agreeableness (36.89) and past exposure to violence/harassment introduce slight skepticism.

peer_exemplars.reasoning: Strong libertarian anti-state control and low conscientiousness oppose compulsory voting, but high civic engagement and fairness values create mild ambivalence.

High libertarian anti-censorship (76.59) and skepticism of state control (67.25) lean against compulsory voting as coercive, but high voting participation (73.67) and voter registration engagement (97.84) create mild ambivalence, resulting in mild opposition.
Very high libertarian anti-state-control orientation and low agreeableness lead to clear opposition to mandatory voting as coercive.

Mild opposition due to very low libertarianism (0.69) and high authoritarian submission (89.94) which normally favor state mandates, but low political efficacy (45.01) and very low voting participation (15.54) reduce enthusiasm.
My libertarian streak and low trust in government make me uneasy about forcing participation, though my high civic engagement tempers outright opposition.

High libertarian privacy concerns (68.54) and low authoritarian submission (3.08) oppose mandatory participation, despite high voting participation (98.71) and a sense of civic duty.
